questions.question: Explain the critical section problem and state its three requirements.
keyPoints: Critical section is code accessing shared resources
Mutual exclusion: only one process in CS at a time
Progress: processes not in remainder section can participate
Bounded waiting: limit on number of times other processes can enter after request

questions.question: Describe the producer-consumer problem and explain how semaphores solve it.
keyPoints: Producer creates items, consumer removes items
Shared bounded buffer problem
Three semaphores: empty, full, mutex
empty initialized to buffer size, full to 0, mutex to 1

questions.question: What is a semaphore? Differentiate between binary and counting semaphores.
keyPoints: Semaphore is an integer variable with atomic wait and signal
Binary: values 0 and 1 only, used for mutual exclusion
Counting: values 0 to n, controls multiple identical resources
wait() decrements, signal() increments

questions.question: State and explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock.
keyPoints: Mutual exclusion: resource can be used by only one process
Hold and wait: process holds resources while waiting for others
No preemption: resources cannot be forcibly taken
Circular wait: circular chain of processes waiting for resources

questions.question: Explain the difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance.
keyPoints: Prevention: eliminate one necessary condition permanently
Avoidance: allow all conditions but make safe allocation decisions
Banker's algorithm is an avoidance technique
Prevention restricts resource usage; avoidance needs future request knowledge

questions.question: What is Peterson's solution for the critical section problem? Explain with the shared variables used.
keyPoints: Software solution for two processes
Uses two shared variables: flag[2] and turn
flag[i] indicates process i wants to enter CS
turn indicates whose turn it is
Provides mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting

questions.question: Explain the dining philosophers problem and discuss how deadlock can occur.
keyPoints: Five philosophers, five forks, need two to eat
Each picks left fork then right fork
Deadlock occurs if all pick up left fork simultaneously
All wait indefinitely for right fork
Solutions: ordering resources, allowing one to pick differently, philosopher states

questions.question: Explain the readers-writers problem. Discuss the different approaches to solve it and mention the advantages and disadvantages of each approach.
keyPoints: Readers can access simultaneously, writers need exclusive access
First readers-writers problem: writers starvation possible
Second readers-writers problem: readers starvation possible
Solution using semaphores: mutex, rw_mutex, read_try
Priority can be given to readers or writers
Trade-off between throughput and fairness

questions.question: Explain the Banker's algorithm for deadlock avoidance. How does it determine if a state is safe? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Banker's algorithm checks if allocation keeps system in safe state
Safe state: exists sequence where all processes can complete
Uses available resources, maximum demand, current allocation
Algorithm: try to find safe sequence by simulating allocations
If safe, grant request; otherwise, deny request
Example with processes and resources needed to illustrate

questions.question: What are synchronization hardware instructions? Explain TestAndSet and Swap instructions with pseudocode, showing how they can be used for mutual exclusion.
keyPoints: Hardware provide atomic instructions for synchronization
TestAndSet atomically reads and modifies a location
Swap atomically exchanges values of two variables
Both can implement mutual exclusion (spinlock)
Pseudocode for both instructions
Advantages and disadvantages of spinlocks

questions.question: Discuss various methods for handling deadlocks. Compare deadlock prevention, avoidance, detection, and recovery.
keyPoints: Prevention: eliminate one condition, restricts resource usage
Avoidance: Banker's algorithm, requires knowledge of future requests
Detection: resource allocation graph or algorithm, run periodically
Recovery: abort processes or preempt resources
Comparison based on overhead, restrictions, and applicability
